title: Triplet-assisted leakage during singlet-triplet qubit readout with a quantum point contact
abstract: Quantum point contact readout theory for singlet-triplet qubits in a lateral double quantum dot is extended by including tunneling of triplet configurations into a higher-energy level of the neighboring dot. This additional channel creates energetically allowed leakage pathways that modify the branch-dependent charge and current-noise signatures, even when the Pauli blockade remains effective within the ground-state manifold. The model contains two single-particle levels in each dot. The resulting singlet and triplet block structure is derived together with a Lindblad master equation. Quantum-jump simulations are then used to resolve the dynamics of individual readout events. A complementary Liouvillian steady-state analysis identifies the regime in which tunneling to the excited level qualitatively changes the readout signatures, with the crossover determined by the level spacing.
summary: This paper explores how excited states in a double quantum dot cause leakage during spin-qubit readout. It demonstrates that these additional pathways can create significant low-frequency noise, potentially reversing the expected noise hierarchy between singlet and triplet states. This is crucial for designing high-fidelity readout protocols in semiconductor quantum computing.
main_problem: Investigating how the presence of excited energy levels in a double quantum dot leads to triplet-assisted leakage during singlet-triplet qubit readout.
main_result: The study identifies that excited-level tunneling creates new leakage pathways that can significantly enhance low-frequency noise, potentially causing the triplet branch to become noisier than the singlet branch.
method: The author uses a Lindblad master equation approach, quantum-jump simulations, and Liouvillian steady-state analysis combined with the quantum regression theorem.
model_or_system: A lateral double quantum dot (DQD) with two single-particle levels per dot, capacitively coupled to a Quantum Point Contact (QPC) charge sensor, modeled via a Hamiltonian including interdot tunneling and on-site Coulomb repulsion.
key_observables: QPC current, current-noise power spectrum, Fano factor, and current contrast.
important_parameters: Level spacing (epsilon), QPC bias voltage, tunneling amplitude (t), and on-site charging energy (U).
main_assumptions: Interdot Coulomb repulsion is neglected, spin is treated as a passive degree of freedom, and tunneling amplitudes are simplified to a single value.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the DQD-QPC schematic; Fig 2 illustrates charge configurations and barrier modulation; Fig 3 details the transition schemes and jump-rate matrices; Fig 4 shows quantum-jump trajectories; Fig 5 compares Fano spectra and relaxation times; Fig 6 plots the zero-frequency Fano factor versus level spacing.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the physical system and Hamiltonian decomposition, moves to the derivation of the master equation and jump operators, presents noise analysis via the quantum regression theorem, and concludes with numerical simulations and pathway analysis to identify the origins of leakage noise.
why_interesting: It provides deep insights into the dynamics of open quantum systems, specifically how detector-induced backaction and higher-energy manifolds influence the noise statistics and fidelity of continuous quantum measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig 2. Band diagram illustrating the three relevant DQD charge configurations and the corresponding QPC detector barrier. Panels (a)–(c) show, respectively, both electrons localized in the left dot, one electron in each dot, and both electrons localized in the right dot. Since the QPC is placed next to the right dot, increasing the right-dot occupation modifies the barrier between the source and drain leads.

Fig 1. Schematic representation of the lateral double quantum dot capacitively coupled to the QPC detector placed next to the right dot. At the top: single particle electron energy levels [cf. Eq. (2)] with an exemplary charge separated configuration in ground mani- fold.

Fig 3. QPC-induced transitions and aggregated jump-rate matrices in the reduced symmetry sectors. Panel (a) shows the complete high-bias singlet graph of off-diagonal QPC-induced transitions between reduced singlet eigenstates; panel (c) shows schematic QPC-induced pathways in the triplet sector; and panels (b) and (d) show the corresponding aggregated jump-rate matrices for 𝜖= 40 meV and 𝜖= 20 meV on a common color scale within each symmetry sector. In the schematic panels, darker arrow saturations denote transitions within the same manifold, intermediate saturations denote transitions between adjacent manifolds, and the lightest saturation denotes transitions between remote manifolds;...

Fig 4. Representative quantum-jump trajectories for post-selected singlet and triplet branches. Left: 𝜖= 40 meV, i.e., above the QPC bias. Right: 𝜖= 20 meV, i.e., below the QPC bias. Each panel shows the corresponding occupation dynamics together with the detector current record for a single representative trajectory in the singlet and triplet branches. The plotted QPC current is shown as a time histogram — counted QPC events are summed in 120 equal-width bins and divided by the bin width, corresponding to Δ𝑡≃0.11 ns per displayed current bin.

Fig 6. Zero-frequency Fano factor 𝐹(0) as a function of the level spacing 𝜖. The singlet branch (solid vermilion line) and the triplet branch (dashed blue line) are shown together. The dark-gray dotted curves show reduced connected pathway models constructed from the dominant shelving transitions and weak connector channels se- lected by the modal pathway ranking of Sec. III. Both reduced models also retain the elastic background channel needed to close the dynam- ics. The inset highlights the range where the singlet remains super- Poissonian while the triplet has already relaxed back to Poissonian behavior.
